The word sky comes from the Old Norse ský, meaning "cloud" or "cover"—a fitting name for the shifting layer that wraps our planet, never still, never empty.<[silence:100ms]>

Most see the sky as blue, but it’s really a filter.<[silence:100ms]> Sunlight scatters off air molecules like a flashlight beam hitting dust:<[silence:80ms]> shorter blue waves bounce wildly, while longer red waves slice through.<[silence:100ms]> At dawn or dusk, sunlight cuts sideways through thicker air, bleeding reds and oranges—like stretching a paintbrush stroke until only the darkest hues remain.<[silence:100ms]>

clean_story:
Earth’s blanket. Light’s canvas.

The word sky comes from the Old Norse ský, meaning "cloud" or "cover"—a fitting name for the shifting layer that wraps our planet, never still, never empty.

Most see the sky as blue, but it’s really a filter. Sunlight scatters off air molecules like a flashlight beam hitting dust: shorter blue waves bounce wildly, while longer red waves slice through. At dawn or dusk, sunlight cuts sideways through thicker air, bleeding reds and oranges—like stretching a paintbrush stroke until only the darkest hues remain.

The sky isn’t just color. It’s weight. The air above you presses down with 14.7 pounds per square inch at sea level—like balancing a bowling ball on your palm, except you never feel it. Birds exploit this: a golden eagle’s wingspan funnels air into lift, turning gravity into a tool. Storms do the opposite, dragging 10 million tons of water upward in a single thunderhead, then dropping it as hail or rain at 20 miles per hour.

Even "empty" sky teems with unseen labor. The ionosphere, 50 miles up, bounces radio waves like a mirror, letting AM stations broadcast beyond the horizon. Auroras flicker there too, when solar wind slams into Earth’s magnetic field—a collision so violent it paints the night green.

Three related subjects are atmospheric layers, how clouds form, and why sunsets look different.
